I benchmarked some variants of this a bit (it is the inner loop of the decoder after all) and here's the fastest thing I came up with (I was experimenting only with variants of the byte-iteration logic, not the shift/mask expressions, FWIW):

	if sys.version_info.major== 2:
		def decode_uvarint(data):
			n = 0
			shift = 0
			i = 0
			for b in data:
				b = ord(b)
				i += 1
				if b < 0x80:
					return n | b << shift, i
				n |= (b & 0x7f) << shift
				shift += 7
			raise Exception('cannot read uvarint from buffer')
	else:
		def decode_uvarint(data):
			n = 0
			shift = 0
			i = 0
			for b in data:
				i += 1
				if b < 0x80:
					return n | b << shift, i
				n |= (b & 0x7f) << shift
				shift += 7
			raise Exception('cannot read uvarint from buffer')

Using the above code makes it about twice as fast to decode the varints in a typical macaroon as the currently proposed approach.
My benchmark experimentations are here: http://paste.ubuntu.com/25363833/

With python 2, this code takes about 12µs to decode a macaroon's elements; the above code takes about 6.7µs.
With python 3, the times are 12µs and 7.5µs respectively.

So perhaps worth doing.

In the first time update, the 'self.num_update' used in index_0 is 0 and 1 for other index. The same wrong will occur in the next update loop. 
So when lr for indexes except index_0 changed in the function self.lr_scheduler, the index_0 will get the new lr in the next loop.

While I don't have any particular objection for adding those 2 test cases (and I've updated this PR already), I would like to take this chance to say, I am worrying those new test cases and (especially) our tendency of "never break existing test cases", will further limit our future options on a potential paginator refactoring.

To better understand the background, let's imagine that we were still in day 1 of designing the paginator, it would be a matter of taste to decide whether an absent (primary or secondary) result_key should show up as the corresponding empty type (i.e. my previous [commit](https://github.com/rayluo/botocore/commit/ccdbcfc778ded1060e3db68f6f9e9c529afa0e14)), or not show up at all (i.e. my first [commit](https://github.com/rayluo/botocore/commit/a3e70de5e76f9f77806c959bb04ffe0ee8a04a3f)). In other words, these behavior was an implementation detail. And, implementation detail "should not impact API" in the first place. (There is a good explanation in slide 15 of this keynote http://lcsd05.cs.tamu.edu/slides/keynote.pdf and BTW, this is the webcast http://www.infoq.com/presentations/effective-api-design). If now it becomes a rigid part of our api contract and then limits our flexibility of refactoring, hence unnoticeably increases the complexity (thus the workload) on subsequent bug fix/new feature development, that doesn't feel right.

I'm NOT advocating it is OK to break any existing test cases. I'm just hoping we either: (a) do not put implementation detail into test cases, or; (b) or allow us to adjust those implementation level test cases when needed. Besides, we can make it clear that, all those response examples in documentation are merely demonstrating the format of each element, it does NOT hint each element will show up.

PS: This can be considered as a separated conversation. It should not delay or block the review of this PR.

This doesn't work with the current API. We don't have a way to `set_call_context()` (the complete API was proposed to OpenTracing but we never made that change for ourselves), so it means that we can't:
```python
ctx = Context(trace_id=trace_id, parent_id=parent_id, sampling_priority= sampling_priority)
tracer.set_call_context(ctx)  # we don't have this API
```

This means that `context.get_context_attributes()` returns always the value of the first created `Context` when you `start_span()` or `trace()`. Because the `Context` is mutable, to get the right values users should:
```python
ctx = tracer.get_call_context()
ctx.set_context_attributes(...)
# use start_span() or trace() as usual
```

Of course the fact that `Context` is mutable, is something that is planned to be changed.

def _sparsifyVector(self, inputPattern, doWinners=False):

    # Do sparsification, using a relative or absolute threshold
    if not self.relativeThreshold:
      inputPattern = inputPattern*(abs(inputPattern) > self.sparseThreshold)
    elif self.sparseThreshold > 0:
      inputPattern = inputPattern * \
        (abs(inputPattern) > (self.sparseThreshold * abs(inputPattern).max()))

    # Do winner-take-all
    if doWinners:
      if (self.numWinners>0) and (self.numWinners < (inputPattern > 0).sum()):
        sparseInput = numpy.zeros(inputPattern.shape)
        
        # Don't consider strongly negative numbers as winners.
        sorted = inputPattern.argsort()[0:self.numWinners]
        sparseInput[sorted] += inputPattern[sorted]
        inputPattern = sparseInput

    # Do binarization
    if self.doBinarization:
      # Don't binarize negative numbers to positive 1.
      inputPattern = (inputPattern > self.binarizationThreshold).astype(float)

    return inputPattern

My recollection was that this was a substantial effect for the Great3 variable PSF sims, which we don't have currently in the examples/great3 directory.  But we can mock it up with cgc by re-making a PSF for each object with psf.index_key=obj_num.  Here are some timings.

On this branch:
```
$ galsim cgc.yaml output.nfiles=1 output.noclobber=False psf.index_key=obj_num image.nx_tiles=1
File 0 = control/ground/constant/image-000-0.fits: time = 30.303779 sec
$ galsim cgc.yaml output.nfiles=1 output.noclobber=False psf.index_key=obj_num image.nx_tiles=1 psf.items.1.max_size=1.e500
File 0 = control/ground/constant/image-000-0.fits: time = 30.807760 sec
```

On master:
```
$ galsim cgc.yaml output.nfiles=1 output.noclobber=False psf.index_key=obj_num image.nx_tiles=1
File 0 = control/ground/constant/image-000-0.fits: time = 23.059284 sec
$ galsim cgc.yaml output.nfiles=1 output.noclobber=False psf.index_key=obj_num image.nx_tiles=1 psf.items.1.max_size=1.e500
File 0 = control/ground/constant/image-000-0.fits: time = 29.484686 sec
```

So at least for the kind of application this parameter was designed for, the new implementation doesn't help.  The idea was really to speed up the PSF image generation process by not making as large an image.  

But if you think this is invalid, I'll take your word for it, since you understand this stuff much better than I do.  In that case, I'd recommend just deprecating max_size and have the message say that the previous use to speed up the calculation may lead to aliasing problems, so we removed it, but if you don't mind the aliasing, then you can speed up the calculation similarly by lowering pad_factor below the default 1.5.

since there is inheritance, only one instance is enough. using `super`, you can create an instance that behaves like parent class but share all the states with child class. for example:
```python
In [1]: class A(object):
   ...:     def __init__(self, v):
   ...:         self.v = v
   ...:     def get(self):
   ...:         return 'A' + str(self.v)
   ...:

In [2]: class B(A):
   ...:     def get(self):
   ...:         return 'B' + str(self.v)
   ...:

In [3]: b = B(123)

In [4]: a = super(B, b)

In [5]: b.get()
Out[5]: 'B123'

In [6]: a.get()
Out[6]: 'A123'
```

I'm not clear on the difference between `prune` and `recursive-exclude`. The [documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/sourcedist.html#manifest) doesn't suggest that there should be any difference between the two commands. The only difference I can see is that `recursive-exclude` requires that we specify every different kind of file -- for `loadtests`, this would be `.ini` files, `.py` files, `.json`, and a Makefile. For `talks`, it would be `.rst`, `.css`, a couple stray `.cfg`s, some `.png`s, `.jpg`s, and `.svg`s, and another Makefile. It seems to me like `prune` suggests that no matter what file shows up in `talks`, we don't want it in the package, but `recursive-exclude` suggests that we only want to exclude certain kinds of files and other kinds of files in `talks` would be OK. > Is that actually what the generated library enforces? E.g. the user can't pass 0 for required numbers, "" for required strings, and [] for required bytes?

No, it doesn't enforce the restriction. It just makes those parameters mandatory in languages (like Python) that distinguish required from keyword/optional arguments, making it less likely for users to leave them unset accidentally. AFAIK, there's no technical reason that we couldn't validate, though.

So immediately after calling mkstemp, you need to call os.close(fd):

```Python
fd, filename = tempfile.mkstemp(prefix='cmd2_test', suffix='.txt')
os.close(fd)
```

Python's `PyMem_New` wraps malloc itself, but also keeps its own pool of free memory, so it has the potential to be closer in memory to the PyObject itself.  It may perform better in a tight allocate-deallocate loop (with Python reusing memory rather than needing to dealloc/malloc).  It doesn't mix with C++ allocation via new/delete, but in this case, where we just need a small chunk of memory that is controlled entirely within pybind, it seems appropriate.
